chain_of_thought: Top native frame is stream_gets in libmainstone.so, but symbol not resolved; next frame ms_decode_buf_pnm, then MsDecodeBuffer wrapper in libmainstone/libbcr calling a function pointer PTR_MsDecodeBuffer_0068aed8.

Decompiled JNI entry Java_com_intsig_nativelib_BCREngine_RecognizeCardData in libbcr.so shows it obtains a jbyteArray (param_3) from Java, gets its length (uVar2) and elements pointer (uVar4), and calls MsDecodeBuffer(uVar4, uVar2, 4, &local_60). The third argument is constant 4 (likely pixel format or channels), so attacker controls only buffer pointer and length.

MsDecodeBuffer in libbcr.so is just an indirection to PTR_MsDecodeBuffer_0068aed8, whose implementation is likely in libmainstone.so (ms_decode_buf_pnm, stream_gets). The crash termination reason is 'stream_gets', suggesting an internal routine reading from the provided buffer and likely treating it as an image/PNM stream; without bounds checks inside that implementation, malformed data could trigger memory issues or internal asserts.

However, the immediate crash reason is just 'Process Termination : stream_gets' with no explicit evidence of heap or stack corruption; it may be a defensive abort inside the library when encountering invalid format, similar to fgets-like safe reading or custom error handler. There is no direct unsafe memcpy/memmove seen at the JNI frame; MsDecodeBuffer is a black box behind a function pointer.

Attack-controlled data clearly flows from the Java byte[] into MsDecodeBuffer, but without visibility into ms_decode_buf_pnm/stream_gets internals, we cannot assert memory corruption vs. intentional abort. Given typical imaging libs, "stream_gets" could be a safe wrapper that aborts on protocol violation, yielding only a local DoS in this app when fed invalid image bytes (which fuzzing does).

Because triage policy distinguishes genuine vulnerabilities (memory safety, logic flaws) from benign DoS due to malformed input, and here the only observed effect is a clean termination by an internal function name (no sanitizer backtrace, no invalid free, etc.), this is best classified as a non-vulnerability / robust error handling under fuzzed input.

Nevertheless, there is a realistic local DoS angle: untrusted images reaching RecognizeCardData could still crash the app if the library calls abort on bad data. But absent proof of memory corruption or broader impact, severity is low and confidence that there is *no* memory safety bug is moderate, not absolute.
reasons: JNI bridge passes attacker-controlled byte[] and length directly into MsDecodeBuffer via function pointer, but third arg is constant and JNI code itself appears safe
Crash reason points to internal stream_gets abort without explicit signs of memory corruption or allocator/sanitizer involvement
Lack of visibility into ms_decode_buf_pnm/stream_gets prevents proving memory-safety issues; behaviour is consistent with defensive termination on invalid image data

evidence.note: JNI obtains byte[] length and elements then forwards directly into MsDecodeBuffer with constant format argument.
Wrapper around function pointer, likely implemented in libmainstone; internals unknown.

recommendations: Inspect and decompile ms_decode_buf_pnm/stream_gets implementation in libmainstone.so to confirm whether it uses safe bounds-checked parsing or contains over-reads/writes.
Instrument MsDecodeBuffer/stream_gets with logging or error codes instead of hard process termination so malformed images do not crash the app.
Add higher-level validation of image buffers in Java or JNI (e.g., basic header/size checks) before calling RecognizeCardData to reduce exposure to malformed data.
assumptions: stream_gets name corresponds to an internal parsing/error routine and the observed termination is not accompanied by allocator/sanitizer corruption reports.
Byte[] passed into RecognizeCardData originates from attacker-influenced image data (e.g., camera or external files).
limitations: No decompiled body available for stream_gets or ms_decode_buf_pnm, so memory safety properties of the core decoder are unknown.
Crash log lacks low-level fault info (no SIGSEGV/SIGABRT context), so exact failure mechanism inside stream_gets cannot be determined.
